author: @lifescholar5887
text:
Great content! And lucid explanation :)
The idea that you shared here of Magnetically geared machines is actually being researched. The ones that I know are radial machines unlike yours, which is axial. You can search for the terms "Partitioned Stator Flux Switching or Flux Reversal PM Machines". 
The outer stator consists of Armature winding (similar to PMSM or IM) And an inner stator with PM mounted on them(either surface or spoke or V type). You can think of inner stator as rotor of PMSM but stationary. And in between those stators is rotating steel pieces(and the number of pieces is the sum of pole pairs of inner and outer poles pairs, just like you explained here). You can imagine the shape of rotor piece as the pieces of KITKAT.  They work as modulator of flux. And as compared to PMSM the torque is density a relatively high of these machines due to the use of inner empty space. However the leakage flux is pretty high through the rotor pieces.

Maybe I'll try to make an FEA model of the axial type machine as well.

author: @tiger12506
text: It's an offset. Imagine the two outer discs circumference on a number line. So you have a line of 7 equally spaced and a line of 8 equally spaced (like a vernier caliper). They only align on one set of magnets at any given time. As you shift one, the pair that aligns changes (just like the calipers). The direction is based on which pair can align closest -- if it were a straight line, it would always be one direction, but on the discs it can wrap around and be closest in the opposite direction under certain distance conditions.

author: @TheRebelmanone
text:
Very interesting. Could be made into a electronic clutch too, wear free clutch plate because  the plate is a magnetic field. If you want it to gradually kick in, then use less electricity to the clutch coil initially, then climb to full power after it starts catching. IT could be a clutch/reducer all in one.

It is interesting, but the permanent magnet only has so much stored energy therefore the torque it can provide is set. I don't know the technical words to use, but the permanent magnet is only so strong so if you put a load on the center disk like you were saying, if it is too heavy of a load the magnetic fields in those magnets will allow the reducer to slip. It is awesome ideal, but only good for small stuff in the case of using permanent magnets. 

You could use electromagnets this way you can generate a bigger magnetic field that can drive big loads. But the electromagnetic design would need to be huge coils and iron plates in order to have that huge torque needed to not allow the disc to slip in the magnetic field..

That's quite interesting.

If you put cogs on the external portion of the discs, you can use a levered cogs to determine which of the discs is held in place. 

If you have a sliding gear such as in a manual transmission, then you can choose which of the three discs to turn giving you a reversible transmission in a small little package.

If you add electromagnetic clutches to them, then you may be able to have more than three discs giving you all sorts of nifty variations. But that is pure speculation and would have to be proven through experimentation.

author: @GnuReligion
text: It may be interesting if the center (rotor) portion of the motor could change its shape on the fly.  Perhaps an abundant (highly divisible) number of mini-bolts, flat, can be mechanically clustered.  If there are 24 ferrite leaves, they can be grouped into 2,3,4,6,8,12, or 24, yielding 7 ratios.  I struggle to imagine a good mechanical means to move the leaves though.  One way could be to make 24 grooves on the inside of a pipe (drive shaft) that slides over the rotor, clustering the leaves in various patterns.  You may need the help of a 3d printer to construct a thing like this.

replies.author: @williamfraser
replies.text: I demonstrated this in one of my videos by shining light through cardboard templates. One disc represents the gear with many magnets - I had cut out only holes for one polarity of each pole pair. On a second disc I punched out holes that would represent the bolts. When I aligned them axially and held them up against a bright light, the modified field on the pinion's side was clearly defined. If you turn either of the two discs while keeping the other stationary, you will also see the modified field rotate at a higher rpm.

replies.author: @traininggrounds9450
replies.text: @Heads Mess Magnets are simply aligned protons and electrons. The more alignment you get, the stronger the magnet. Electricity and magnetism come from positive and negative charges. Positive alignment in one direction creates the south pole and negative alignment in the other direction creates the north pole. But they are simply aligned spins of charges along similar axes which gives the lines of force you see in the iron filings display. So electricity is just moving electron flow and magnetism is static electron, proton alignment. There is no hidden mechanism. No hidden particles. Electricity and magnetism arise out of the same phenomenon of the attractive force between  positive and negatively charged particles which are "charged" through their spin states.
